brief_document.title: InMemoryStore reference implementation
brief_document.purpose: Provide the correct, non-durable reference implementation of EventStore. It is the behavioural oracle every durable backend must match and the store the engine's own tests use.
brief_document.task: Implement InMemoryStore: a Send + Sync EventStore backed by in-memory maps behind a lock. It must implement every trait method correctly, including the atomic append with expected-sequence guard and durable-timer scheduling and retrieval. Correctness matters more than performance — this is the oracle.

requirements.title: InMemoryStore structure and event methods
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL implement InMemoryStore as an EventStore backed by in-memory state behind a lock, implementing append, read_history, list_active, and query.
acceptance: memory.rs defines InMemoryStore holding per-workflow event vectors and any derived index behind a Mutex or RwLock; the struct is Send + Sync and Default-constructible.
read_history returns the stored events in sequence order, and an empty Vec (never NotFound) for an unknown workflow, per the AC-005 contract.
list_active returns workflows whose projected status (via aion-core status projection) is Running.
query evaluates each workflow's summary against the filter using the aion-core filter semantics.
Lock poisoning is handled explicitly (no unwrap that can panic on a poisoned lock).

requirements.title: Atomic append with sequence guard
requirements.spec: WHEN append is called with an expected sequence equal to the stored head, THE SYSTEM SHALL apply all events atomically and advance the head. WHEN the expected sequence does not match, THE SYSTEM SHALL apply no events and return SequenceConflict.
acceptance: append checks the current head sequence for the workflow against expected_seq before applying.
On match, all events in the batch are appended together and the head advances by the batch length.
On mismatch, no events are applied and StoreError::SequenceConflict { expected, found } is returned.
A test appends two batches in order (success), then attempts an append with a stale expected_seq and asserts SequenceConflict and that no partial write occurred.
Concurrency test: two appends racing on the same expected_seq result in exactly one success and one SequenceConflict.

requirements.title: Durable timer implementation
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL implement schedule_timer and expired_timers in InMemoryStore so that scheduled timers are retrievable once due.
acceptance: schedule_timer records a TimerEntry keyed by (workflow_id, timer_id).
expired_timers(as_of) returns all entries with fire_at <= as_of and does not return future timers.
Re-scheduling the same timer id replaces the prior entry (idempotent under replay).
A test schedules timers at different instants and asserts expired_timers returns exactly the due ones for a given as_of.

boundaries: SHALL NOT add durability (file or database) — durability is clusters AS and AX.
SHALL NOT optimise for performance at the cost of obvious correctness — this is the reference oracle.
SHALL NOT define the shared behavioural test suite — that is AC-007 (this brief provides the implementation it runs against).
verification: Mutex/RwLock poisoning handled explicitly per CLAUDE.md.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: InMemoryStore implements EventStore in full, including timer scheduling and retrieval.
InMemoryStore append enforces the expected-sequence guard and applies events atomically (all or none).

stories.text: As a store author, I want an in-memory reference implementation and a shared behavioural test suite so that I can verify my backend matches the contract exactly.

constraints.text: lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
aion-core and aion-store are leaf crates — they depend on no other Aion crate. Structural; must hold.
Every type that crosses the wire or the store derives Serialize + Deserialize (these are data types, not credentials).
WorkflowStatus is always derivable from event history; no independent status mutation.
InMemoryStore must pass the same behavioural test suite future durable backends are held to — it is the oracle, so it must be correct.
Identifiers are newtypes; no bare Uuid or String in public signatures where an ID is meant.

resolved_context.intention: Define the vocabulary every other part of Aion speaks: the event types, identifiers, workflow status, error taxonomy, and the persistence contract. When the engine records an activity completion, a client lists running workflows, a store persists history, or a worker reports a result, they all use the types defined here. The types must make illegal states unrepresentable — a completed workflow cannot also be running, an activity result cannot exist without a preceding schedule. Nothing here depends on beamr, the engine, networking, or any storage backend. This is the still point at the centre: pure types and one trait, so the rest of Aion can be built against stable contracts.
